citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
The facility did not follow the Program of Vet Care (PVC) as directed. The facility treated a goat with an abscess and gave
Banamine, a pain reliever, underneath the skin (also known as subcutaneously). The PVC prescribes for this medication
to be given intravenously only. The APHIS inspectors conferred with the Attending Veterinarian and he confirmed that the
only route he has authorized for Banamine to be given is intravenously (IV). The PVC outlines appropriate methods to
prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. If the PVC is not properly followed, expected medical
outcomes may not occur, may worsen, or a new issue may arise. Correct by ensuring each research facility establishes
and maintains programs of veterinary care that include the use of appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and
treat diseases and injuries. Correct by May 18, 2026.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
During the inspection, the following animals were observed to be in need of veterinary medical care. These animals were
not identified by facility personnel prior to the inspection:
-An adult goat with ear tag # 2235 was observed to be limping while in motion.
-An adult goat with ear tag # 378 was found to have excessive amounts of discharge from both nostrils. The discharge on
the left side was noticeably worse covering most of the nostril. The discharge appeared thick and white/yellow in color
with crusting formed along the edges.
Failure to conduct adequate daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the animals with direct and frequent
communication to the attending veterinarian can delay treatment leading to the condition getting worse and delays in
healing. Each research facility shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily
observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being and that a mechanism of direct and frequent
communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being
is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. Correct by ensuring that these animals are examined and treated by a licensed
veterinarian. Correct by close of business 13-MAY-2025.

The outdoor housing facilities are not being adequately maintained in good repair, may not protect the animals from injury,
and do not contain the animals. At the Scott Road location on the southern side of the pasture, there is an area of fencing
that contains sharp points and a gap between the fence and the ground. There is an approximately 12 inch gap between
the fencing and the ground, which may not contain the animals. There is a portion of the fencing that has sharp points on
the ends of the metal panels. At the Harris Road facility, there was one adult goat and one juvenile goat seen outside of
their enclosures and freely moving about outside the facility. The goats were all safely returned to their enclosures by staff
members.
Failure to maintain the housing facilities in good repair could result in injury and may not contain the animals. The facility
must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. Correct by ensuring
that outdoor housing facilities are structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and
to contain the animals.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Jonathan Tomkovitch, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

2.33(b)(3)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care. 
During the inspection, the following animals were observed to be in need of veterinary medical care. These animals were 
not identified by facility personnel prior to the inspection: 
 
-An adult goat with ear tag # 2235 was observed to be limping while in motion. 
 
-An adult goat with ear tag # 378 was found to have excessive amounts of discharge from both nostrils. The discharge on

Failure to conduct adequate daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the animals with direct and frequent

3.125(a)  Repeat
   
Facilities, general. 
The outdoor housing facilities are not being adequately maintained in good repair, may not protect the animals from injury, 
and do not contain the animals. At the Scott Road location on the southern side of the pasture, there is an area of fencing 
that contains sharp points and a gap between the fence and the ground. There is an approximately 12 inch gap between 
the fencing and the ground, which may not contain the animals. There is a portion of the fencing that has sharp points on 
the ends of the metal panels. At the Harris Road facility, there was one adult goat and one juvenile goat seen outside of 
their enclosures and freely moving about outside the facility. The goats were all safely returned to their enclosures by staff 
members.  
 
Failure to maintain the housing facilities in good repair could result in injury and may not contain the animals. The facility 
must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. Correct by ensuring 
that outdoor housing facilities are structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and 
to contain the animals. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors:
